title: The part that compares itself to everyone and always comes up short
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
There's a part of you that watches 

 It watches other people. Their careers. Their relationships. The way they seem to move through life with a kind of e...
htmlContent:
There's a part of you that watches

It watches other people. Their careers. Their relationships. The way they seem to move through life with a kind of ease you can't quite name. And then it turns inward and measures what it sees. And every single time, the measurement comes up short.

I know this part. I've felt its presence in my chest during conversations, in the pit of my stomach when I scroll through social media, in the way my shoulders tighten when someone else succeeds at something I've been trying to do. It's a specific kind of ache. A familiar one.

This part doesn't just compare. It compares and then concludes. It says "they're better" and "you're behind" and "what's wrong with you?" It uses the same old evidence, the same old memories, the same old stories about why you don't measure up. And it does this so automatically that you barely notice it's happening. You just feel the drop. The deflation. The familiar sinking into "not enough."

Here's the thing. This part isn't your enemy. It's not a flaw in your character or a sign that you're broken. It's a protector. A very young, very tired protector that learned a specific job a long time ago and has been doing it ever since.

Think about it. If you grew up in an environment where love was conditional, where attention was scarce, where approval depended on performance, then this part learned that survival meant being better. Being good enough. Being the one who didn't get left behind. Comparison became a survival strategy. A way to scan the environment for threats and adjust accordingly. If someone else was doing better, that meant danger. That meant you might lose what little safety you had.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems therapy, would call this part a manager. It's trying to manage your life so you don't get hurt. It's trying to keep you small enough to stay safe but successful enough to be acceptable. It's a impossible job. And it's been working overtime for years.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office and saying "I just feel like I'm behind everyone else." She asked me "behind who?" And I couldn't name a single person. I couldn't point to a specific metric. I just had this pervasive sense of being less than, of being late to the party, of having missed some crucial instruction manual that everyone else received at birth. That's the part talking. Not reality.

Stay with me here. When you feel that comparison rise up, notice what happens in your body. Does your chest tighten? Does your stomach drop? Do your shoulders curl forward? This is the part's physical signature. It's been living in your body for so long that it feels like just the way things are. But it's not. It's a response. A patterned, learned, protective response.

What this part is actually protecting

Underneath the comparison, underneath the feeling of coming up short, there's something more vulnerable. Usually shame. Usually a younger part that learned it wasn't good enough, wasn't lovable enough, wasn't worthy of care just for existing. The comparing part is the guard at the gate. It keeps that younger part hidden because it believes that if that shame were ever fully seen, you wouldn't survive it.

But here's the paradox. The comparing part is actually the thing that keeps the shame alive. Every time it compares and finds you lacking, it reinforces the original wound. It says "yes, you were right to feel not good enough. Look, here's more evidence." It's a closed loop. A system that keeps itself going because it doesn't know any other way to operate.

Pete Walker, who wrote extensively about complex trauma and its lasting effects, described something similar in his work. He talked about how the inner critic develops as a survival mechanism in neglectful or abusive environments. The critic tries to anticipate criticism from others by criticizing yourself first. It's a preemptive strike. If you can find your flaws before someone else does, maybe you can fix them. Maybe you can avoid the rejection. Maybe you can stay safe.

The comparing part is a close cousin to that inner critic. It's scanning the social for threats. For people who are doing better, who are more successful, who are more loved. And it's using that information to beat you up preemptively. To keep you in line. To make sure you don't get too comfortable or too confident, because that's when the rug gets pulled out from under you.

I've worked with this part in myself for years. And what I've found is that it responds to curiosity, not to force. You can't argue with it. You can't reason it away. You can't tell it "I'm actually good enough" and expect it to believe you. It's been doing this job too long. It knows too much. It has too much evidence on its side.

But you can get curious. You can say "I see you. I see how hard you're working. I see how afraid you are that I'll get hurt if you stop comparing. Tell me about that fear." And when you do, something shifts. The part softens. It might not stop comparing overnight. But it starts to trust that you're paying attention. That you're not going to just let it run the show without noticing.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker breaks down exactly how these internal systems develop and offers concrete ways to start relating to them differently. It's not about getting rid of the comparing part. It's about understanding it.

Why the wellness industry makes this worse

Here's where I get direct. The wellness industry loves the comparing part. It feeds on it. Think about all the messages you've absorbed about "leveling up," about "becoming your best self," about "unlocking your potential." These are not neutral messages. They are messages that assume you are currently not enough. That you need to be fixed. That there's a version of you that is better, and you should be chasing that version.

This is toxic positivity dressed up in expensive leggings and green juice. It's the comparing part's dream come true. A whole industry dedicated to telling you that you should be more, do more, have more. That your worth is conditional on your output. That if you just try harder, you'll finally arrive at a place where you feel okay.

But here's the truth. You will never arrive. The comparing part will always find a new metric. A new person to measure against. A new standard you're failing to meet. Because that's its job. It's not interested in your arrival. It's interested in your survival. And survival means staying vigilant. Staying in the game. Never letting your guard down.

I've stopped chasing "better." Not because I've given up. But because I've realized that the chase itself was the problem. The comparing part was using improvement as a way to stay in control. Every goal I set, every achievement I chased, every milestone I reached was immediately followed by a new comparison. A new feeling of falling short. The finish line kept moving because the finish line was never the point.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why you might have developed a comparing part in the first place. When your emotional needs weren't met, you learned to look outward for validation. And comparison became the tool you used to measure your worth against an impossible standard.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It names something that's hard to name. The absence. The void where emotional attunement should have been. The comparing part often fills that void with activity, with striving, with the endless pursuit of being good enough. But it can never fill it. Because the void isn't about achievement. It's about connection.

How to start relating to this part differently

I'm not going to tell you to stop comparing yourself to others. That's not realistic. The part won't stop just because you want it to. But you can change your relationship to it. You can stop letting it drive the car without you in the driver's seat.

Start small. Next time you notice the comparing part kick in, pause. Just for a breath. Notice the sensation in your body. Name it. "There's the comparing part." That's it. You don't have to argue with it. You don't have to fix it. You just have to notice it.

Then, if you can, get curious. Ask it a question. "What are you afraid would happen if you stopped comparing?" The answer might surprise you. It might be "I'd get lazy." Or "I'd lose everything." Or "I'd be alone." Or "I'd disappear." These are not rational adult fears. These are the fears of a young part that learned the world was dangerous and that your only safety came from being good enough.

When you hear that fear, you can respond with compassion. Not with "that's not true" or "you're being irrational." But with "I hear you. I understand why you're afraid. I'm here now. We're safe." This is not about convincing the part. It's about it. Being present with it. Letting it know it's not alone.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz explains that every part, even the ones that cause us pain, is trying to help. The comparing part is trying to help. It just doesn't know a better way. And that's where you come in. You can offer it a different path.

I've been doing this work for years. And I still have moments where the comparing part flares up. Where I feel the familiar ache of not being enough. But it's different now. I don't spiral. I don't get lost in it for days. I notice it. I name it. I get curious. And then I turn toward something else. Something that matters more than the comparison.

Wild, right? That the thing that felt so huge, so defining, so true for so long can become just a part of your experience instead of the whole of it. The comparing part doesn't disappear. It just takes up less space. It becomes one voice among many instead of the loudest one in the room.

And sometimes, when I'm really present with it, I can feel the fear underneath. The young part that just wanted to be seen. To be held. To be told "you're okay exactly as you are." And I can give that to myself now. Not perfectly. Not all the time. But enough. Enough that the comparing part can rest sometimes. Can let its guard down. Can trust that I've got this.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and healing complex trauma
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay Gibson's validation of childhood experiences with emotionally unavailable parents
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's exploration of childhood emotional neglect
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's accessible introduction to Internal Family Systems therapy

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
